# Atomic Learning — a compiler for inspectable curricula

> *Every lesson step has a receipt. Every claim has a gate.* **Give it an open textbook chapter. It produces an offline learning path where every lesson step can be challenged against the exact source passage it cites.**

> This is the complete plain-text course for AI assistants. Attach or paste it, then ask the assistant to follow prerequisite order and show the source receipts it uses.

## Concepts in prerequisite order

<a id="vectors"></a>

### Vectors

A vector is a fixed-length array whose entries are scalars.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> For current purposes, you can think of a vector as a fixed-length array of scalars.

#### What it is: Vectors as Lists of Numbers

A vector is a fixed-length list of individual numbers, called scalars.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> For current purposes, you can think of a vector as a fixed-length array of scalars.

The numbers in a vector are called its elements, entries, or components.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> As with their code counterparts, we call these scalars the elements of the vector (synonyms include entries and components).

When a vector represents a real-world example, each component can correspond to a meaningful quantity, such as a loan applicant’s income or employment length.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> For example, if we were training a model to predict the risk of a loan defaulting, we might associate each applicant with a vector whose components correspond to quantities like their income, length of employment, or number of previous defaults.

#### Why it exists: Why group related numbers together?

Vectors let one real-world example carry several meaningful measurements together, such as a loan applicant’s income, employment length, and previous defaults.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> For example, if we were training a model to predict the risk of a loan defaulting, we might associate each applicant with a vector whose components correspond to quantities like their income, length of employment, or number of previous defaults.

They also make weighted sums and weighted averages possible through the dot product.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> For example, given some set of values, denoted by a vector $\mathbf{x}  \in \mathbb{R}^n$, and a set of weights, denoted by $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^n$, the weighted sum of the values in $\mathbf{x}$ according to the weights $\mathbf{w}$ could be expressed as the dot product $\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{w}$. When the weights are nonnegative and sum to $1$, i.e., $\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} {w_i} = 1\right)$, the dot product expresses a weighted average.

Vectors can be transformed with a matrix, a calculation used to produce each neural-network layer’s outputs from the previous layer.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> Matrix--vector products also describe the key calculation involved in computing the outputs of each layer in a neural network given the outputs from the previous layer.

#### How it works: How a vector is stored and accessed

A vector holds a fixed number of scalar values in an array.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> For current purposes, you can think of a vector as a fixed-length array of scalars.

Each scalar value is treated as an element of the vector.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> As with their code counterparts, we call these scalars the elements of the vector (synonyms include entries and components).

Code stores a vector as a tensor with one axis.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> Vectors are implemented as $1^{\textrm{st}}$-order tensors.

Code accesses individual elements by their index; in Python, indexing starts at 0.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> Caution: in Python, as in most programming languages, vector indices start at $0$, also known as zero-based indexing, whereas in linear algebra subscripts begin at $1$ (one-based indexing).

<a id="dot-product"></a>

### Dot Product

The dot product sums products of corresponding elements from two vectors.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> Given two vectors $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^d$, their dot product $\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{y}$ (also known as inner product, $\langle \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}  \rangle$) is a sum over the products of the elements at the same position: $\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{y} = \sum_{i=1}^{d} x_i y_i$.

#### What it is: Multiply Matching Entries, Then Add

The dot product of two vectors is found by multiplying entries in the same position and adding those products.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> The dot product of two vectors is a sum over the products of the elements at the same position

A weighted sum can be written as the dot product of a vector of values and a vector of weights.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> the weighted sum of the values in $\mathbf{x}$ according to the weights $\mathbf{w}$ could be expressed as the dot product $\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{w}$.

If the weights are nonnegative and add up to 1, the dot product gives a weighted average.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> When the weights are nonnegative and sum to $1$, i.e., $\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} {w_i} = 1\right)$, the dot product expresses a weighted average.

#### Why it exists: Why combine matching values into one result?

The dot product lets you express a weighted sum of a set of values as one operation.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> given some set of values, denoted by a vector $\mathbf{x}  \in \mathbb{R}^n$, and a set of weights, denoted by $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^n$, the weighted sum of the values in $\mathbf{x}$ according to the weights $\mathbf{w}$ could be expressed as the dot product $\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{w}$.

When the weights are nonnegative and add up to 1, it also expresses a weighted average.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> When the weights are nonnegative and sum to $1$, i.e., $\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} {w_i} = 1\right)$, the dot product expresses a weighted average.

It also provides the basic calculation used in a matrix–vector product: each output entry is a dot product between one matrix row and the vector.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> The matrix--vector product $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$ is simply a column vector of length $m$, whose $i^\textrm{th}$ element is the dot product $\mathbf{a}^\top_i \mathbf{x}$

#### How it works: How two vectors are combined into one number

Multiply the elements that occupy the same position in the two vectors.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> we can calculate the dot product of two vectors by performing an elementwise multiplication followed by a sum:

Add those products together.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> their dot product $\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{y}$ (also known as inner product, $\langle \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}  \rangle$) is a sum over the products of the elements at the same position: $\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{y} = \sum_{i=1}^{d} x_i y_i$.

<a id="matrix-vector-product"></a>

### Matrix–Vector Product

A matrix–vector product produces a vector whose entries are dot products between matrix rows and the input vector.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> The matrix--vector product $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$ is simply a column vector of length $m$, whose $i^\textrm{th}$ element is the dot product $\mathbf{a}^\top_i \mathbf{x}$: $$
> \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}
> = \begin{bmatrix}
> \mathbf{a}^\top_{1} \\
> \mathbf{a}^\top_{2} \\
> \vdots \\
> \mathbf{a}^\top_m \\
> \end{bmatrix}\mathbf{x}
> = \begin{bmatrix}
>  \mathbf{a}^\top_{1} \mathbf{x}  \\
>  \mathbf{a}^\top_{2} \mathbf{x} \\
> \vdots\\
>  \mathbf{a}^\top_{m} \mathbf{x}\\
> \end{bmatrix}.
> $$

#### What it is: Multiplying a Matrix by a Vector

Multiplying matrix A by vector x produces a column vector of length m. Each entry is the dot product of one row of A with x.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> The matrix--vector product $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$ is simply a column vector of length $m$, whose $i^\textrm{th}$ element is the dot product $\mathbf{a}^\top_i \mathbf{x}$

The number of columns in A must equal the length of x.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> Note that the column dimension of A (its length along axis 1) must be the same as the dimension of x (its length).

Multiplying by a matrix can be viewed as a transformation that maps a vector with n entries to one with m entries.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> We can think of multiplication with a matrix $\mathbf{A}\in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ as a transformation that projects vectors from $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ to $\mathbb{R}^{m}$.

#### Why it exists: Why multiply a matrix by a vector?

It provides a way to transform a vector into another vector, which may have a different length.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> We can think of multiplication with a matrix $\mathbf{A}\in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ as a transformation that projects vectors from $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ to $\mathbb{R}^{m}$.

These transformations can represent actions such as rotating a vector.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> For example, we can represent rotations as multiplications by certain square matrices.

They also perform the key calculation that turns one neural-network layer’s outputs into the next layer’s outputs.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> Matrix--vector products also describe the key calculation involved in computing the outputs of each layer in a neural network given the outputs from the previous layer.

#### How it works: How a matrix and vector are multiplied

Take each row of the matrix and calculate its dot product with the input vector.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> whose $i^\textrm{th}$ element is the dot product $\mathbf{a}^\top_i \mathbf{x}$

Place each result into the matching position of a column vector. The finished vector has one entry for each of the matrix’s $m$ rows.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> The matrix--vector product $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$ is simply a column vector of length $m$

<a id="softmax"></a>

### Softmax

Softmax exponentiates a collection of outputs and normalizes them into values that sum to one.

Source: `d2l-softmax-regression`

> Putting these two pieces together gives us the softmax function: $$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathrm{softmax}(\mathbf{o}) \quad \textrm{where}\quad \hat{y}_i = \frac{\exp(o_i)}{\sum_j \exp(o_j)}.$$

#### What it is: Turning scores into normalized values

Softmax exponentiates each input, then divides it by the sum of all the exponentiated inputs.

Source: `d2l-softmax-regression`

> Putting these two pieces together gives us the softmax function: $$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathrm{softmax}(\mathbf{o}) \quad \textrm{where}\quad \hat{y}_i = \frac{\exp(o_i)}{\sum_j \exp(o_j)}.$$

The largest input corresponds to the class that softmax rates as most likely.

Source: `d2l-softmax-regression`

> Note that the largest coordinate of $\mathbf{o}$ corresponds to the most likely class according to $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$.

#### Why it exists: Why model scores need to become probabilities

Using the model’s raw outputs directly is unsatisfactory because they may not add up to 1, as probabilities should.

Source: `d2l-softmax-regression`

> There is no guarantee that the outputs $o_i$ sum up to $1$ in the way we expect probabilities to behave.

Raw outputs may also be negative or greater than 1, making them unsuitable as probabilities.

Source: `d2l-softmax-regression`

> There is no guarantee that the outputs $o_i$ are even nonnegative, even if their outputs sum up to $1$, or that they do not exceed $1$.

A previous approach added normally distributed noise to the outputs, but it did not work as well or produce as nice an optimization problem as softmax.

Source: `d2l-softmax-regression`

> While appealing, it does not work quite as well nor lead to a particularly nice optimization problem, when compared to the softmax.

Softmax exists to turn ordinary model outputs into valid probability distributions over separate categories.

Source: `d2l-softmax-regression`

> As a side effect, we encountered the softmax, a convenient activation function that transforms outputs of an ordinary neural network layer into valid discrete probability distributions.

#### How it works: How softmax changes a set of outputs

Raise the mathematical constant e to the power of each output.

Source: `d2l-softmax-regression`

> Another way to accomplish this goal (and to ensure nonnegativity) is to use an exponential function $P(y = i) \propto \exp o_i$.

Divide each resulting value by the sum of all those values, making them add up to 1.

Source: `d2l-softmax-regression`

> We can then transform these values so that they add up to $1$ by dividing each by their sum.

<a id="qkv"></a>

### Queries, Keys, and Values

An attention database can be represented as key–value pairs that are accessed using a query.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> For now, simply consider the following: denote by $\mathcal{D} \stackrel{\textrm{def}}{=} \{(\mathbf{k}_1, \mathbf{v}_1), \ldots (\mathbf{k}_m, \mathbf{v}_m)\}$ a database of $m$ tuples of keys and values.

#### What it is: Using a Query with Keys and Values

Think of the data as a collection of key–value pairs.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> denote by $\mathcal{D} \stackrel{\textrm{def}}{=} \{(\mathbf{k}_1, \mathbf{v}_1), \ldots (\mathbf{k}_m, \mathbf{v}_m)\}$ a database of $m$ tuples of keys and values.

A query is used to assign an attention weight to each key.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> where $\alpha(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{k}_i) \in \mathbb{R}$ ($i = 1, \ldots, m$) are scalar attention weights.

Attention pooling combines the stored values into a weighted sum.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> As such, the attention over $\mathcal{D}$ generates a linear combination of values contained in the database.

A larger weight means the operation pays more attention to that value.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> The name attention derives from the fact that the operation pays particular attention to the terms for which the weight $\alpha$ is significant (i.e., large).

#### Why it exists: Why use queries, keys, and values?

Networks that process one token at a time can struggle when the amount and content of input vary, especially because long sequences make past information difficult to track.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> This approach can lead to significant problems when the input is truly of varying size with varying information content, such as in in the transformation of text . In particular, for long sequences it becomes quite difficult to keep track of everything that has already been generated or even viewed by the network.

A query can work with key–value pairs no matter how large the collection is.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> We can design queries $q$ that operate on ($k$,$v$) pairs in such a manner as to be valid regardless of the database size.

The collection does not need to be compressed or simplified for queries to work effectively.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> There is no need to compress or simplify the database to make the operations effective.

The query can stay concise even when searching a large collection, so a network layer does not need too many parameters to learn.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> What is quite remarkable is that the actual "code" for executing on the set of keys and values, namely the query, can be quite concise, even though the space to operate on is significant. This is a desirable property for a network layer as it does not require too many parameters to learn.

#### How it works: How a Query Uses Keys and Values

The data is arranged as a database of key–value pairs, and a query is provided.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> denote by $\mathcal{D} \stackrel{\textrm{def}}{=} \{(\mathbf{k}_1, \mathbf{v}_1), \ldots (\mathbf{k}_m, \mathbf{v}_m)\}$ a database of $m$ tuples of keys and values. Moreover, denote by $\mathbf{q}$ a query.

A weight is derived from how compatible the query is with each key.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> weights are derived according to the compatibility between a query $\mathbf{q}$ and keys $\mathbf{k}_\mathit{i}$.

Attention pooling multiplies each value by its weight and adds the results together.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> The attention mechanism computes a linear combination over values $\mathbf{v}_\mathit{i}$ via attention pooling

In the most common deep-learning setting, the weights are at least zero and add up to 1.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> The weights $\alpha(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{k}_i)$ form a convex combination, i.e., $\sum_i \alpha(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{k}_i) = 1$ and $\alpha(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{k}_i) \geq 0$ for all $i$. This is the most common setting in deep learning.

<a id="attention-pooling"></a>

### Attention Pooling

Attention pooling combines values linearly using weights determined by query–key compatibility.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> The attention mechanism computes a linear combination over values $\mathbf{v}_\mathit{i}$ via attention pooling, where weights are derived according to the compatibility between a query $\mathbf{q}$ and keys $\mathbf{k}_\mathit{i}$.

#### What it is: Combining Values by Relevance

Attention pooling makes a linear combination of values. Its weights come from how compatible the query is with each key.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> The attention mechanism computes a linear combination over values $\mathbf{v}_\mathit{i}$ via attention pooling, where weights are derived according to the compatibility between a query $\mathbf{q}$ and keys $\mathbf{k}_\mathit{i}$.

When the weights are nonnegative and add up to 1, large weights can be viewed as the model selecting relevant parts—but this is only an intuition.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> In this case we might interpret large weights as a way for the model to select components of relevance. While this is a good intuition, it is important to remember that it is just that, an intuition.

Attention pooling can work with databases of any size without changing how the operation is performed.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> Just as convenient is the fact that attention can operate on arbitrarily large databases without the need to change the way the attention pooling operation is performed.

#### Why it exists: Why weighted selection is useful

Fixed-size or one-token-at-a-time methods can cause major problems when inputs vary in size and information content, such as text.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> Variable size is addressed by sequentially processing one token at a time, or by specially designed convolution kernels . This approach can lead to significant problems when the input is truly of varying size with varying information content, such as in in the transformation of text .

For long sequences, a network can have trouble keeping track of everything it has seen or generated.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> In particular, for long sequences it becomes quite difficult to keep track of everything that has already been generated or even viewed by the network.

Attention pooling lets a neural network select relevant items from a set and build a weighted sum from them.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> By design, the attention mechanism provides a differentiable means of control by which a neural network can select elements from a set and to construct an associated weighted sum over representations.

It can handle databases of any size without changing how the pooling operation works.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> Just as convenient is the fact that attention can operate on arbitrarily large databases without the need to change the way the attention pooling operation is performed.

#### How it works: How attention pooling combines values

It derives a weight for each value from how compatible the query is with that value’s key.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> weights are derived according to the compatibility between a query $\mathbf{q}$ and keys $\mathbf{k}_\mathit{i}$.

It multiplies each value by its weight and adds the results, producing a linear combination of the values.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> Then we can define the attention over $\mathcal{D}$ as $$\textrm{Attention}(\mathbf{q}, \mathcal{D}) \stackrel{\textrm{def}}{=} \sum_{i=1}^m \alpha(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{k}_i) \mathbf{v}_i,$$

In the most common deep-learning setting, the weights are nonnegative and add up to 1.

Source: `d2l-queries-keys-values`

> The weights $\alpha(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{k}_i)$ form a convex combination, i.e., $\sum_i \alpha(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{k}_i) = 1$ and $\alpha(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{k}_i) \geq 0$ for all $i$. This is the most common setting in deep learning.

<a id="self-attention"></a>

### Self-Attention

Self-attention lets every token attend to the other tokens in the same sequence.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> Because every token is attending to each other token (unlike the case where decoder steps attend to encoder steps), such architectures are typically described as self-attention models , and elsewhere described as intra-attention model .

#### What it is: How Tokens Look at One Another

In self-attention, every token attends to every other token in the same sequence.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> Because every token is attending to each other token (unlike the case where decoder steps attend to encoder steps), such architectures are typically described as self-attention models , and elsewhere described as intra-attention model .

Self-attention produces an output sequence with the same length as the input sequence.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> Given a sequence of input tokens $\mathbf{x}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_n$ where any $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ ($1 \leq i \leq n$), its self-attention outputs a sequence of the same length $\mathbf{y}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{y}_n$

For each token, self-attention uses attention pooling over the tokens in the sequence.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> where $$\mathbf{y}_i = f(\mathbf{x}_i, (\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_1), \ldots, (\mathbf{x}_n, \mathbf{x}_n)) \in \mathbb{R}^d$$ according to the definition of attention pooling in .

#### Why it exists: Why let every token connect directly?

CNNs and RNNs often encoded sequences before attention-based models.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> In deep learning, we often use CNNs or RNNs to encode sequences.

Self-attention gives each token a direct connection to every other token, which allows parallel computation and makes the longest path between tokens as short as possible.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> As we can see from , each token is directly connected to any other token via self-attention. Therefore, computation can be parallel with $\mathcal{O}(1)$ sequential operations and the maximum path length is also $\mathcal{O}(1)$.

This shorter path makes it easier to learn relationships between distant parts of a sequence.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> a shorter path between any combination of sequence positions makes it easier to learn long-range dependencies within the sequence

The tradeoff is that self-attention becomes prohibitively slow for very long sequences because its work grows with the square of sequence length.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> However, the quadratic computational complexity with respect to the sequence length makes self-attention prohibitively slow for very long sequences.

#### How it works: How each token uses the whole sequence

The sequence enters the attention mechanism, where each token has its own query, keys, and values.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> imagine feeding a sequence of tokens into an attention mechanism such that at every step, each token has its own query, keys, and values.

For a token’s next representation, its query can attend to any other token by matching against that token’s keys.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> Here, when computing the value of a token's representation at the next layer, the token can attend (via its query vector) to any other's token (matching based on their key vectors).

For each token, the full set of query-key compatibility scores determines a weighted sum over the other tokens, producing its representation.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> Using the full set of query-key compatibility scores, we can compute, for each token, a representation by building the appropriate weighted sum over the other tokens.

The output is a sequence with the same number of tokens as the input.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> Given a sequence of input tokens $\mathbf{x}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_n$ where any $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ ($1 \leq i \leq n$), its self-attention outputs a sequence of the same length $\mathbf{y}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{y}_n$

<a id="positional-encoding"></a>

### Positional Encoding

Positional encodings provide token-order information to models as additional token-associated inputs.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> The dominant approach for preserving information about the order of tokens is to represent this to the model as an additional input associated with each token.

#### What it is: Adding Token Order Information

The usual way to preserve token order is to give the model an extra input associated with each token.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> The dominant approach for preserving information about the order of tokens is to represent this to the model as an additional input associated with each token.

These extra inputs are called positional encodings, and they may be learned or set in advance.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> These inputs are called positional encodings, and they can either be learned or fixed a priori.

One fixed positional encoding scheme uses sine and cosine functions.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> We now describe a simple scheme for fixed positional encodings based on sine and cosine functions .

The positional encoding is added to the input representation using a positional embedding matrix of the same shape.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> The positional encoding outputs $\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{P}$ using a positional embedding matrix $\mathbf{P} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ of the same shape

#### Why it exists: Why the model needs clues about word order

Self-attention favors parallel work instead of processing tokens one at a time.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> Unlike RNNs, which recurrently process tokens of a sequence one-by-one, self-attention ditches sequential operations in favor of parallel computation.

The problem is that self-attention alone does not keep track of the sequence’s order.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> Note that self-attention by itself does not preserve the order of the sequence.

When order matters, the dominant approach gives the model extra order information tied to each token.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> The dominant approach for preserving information about the order of tokens is to represent this to the model as an additional input associated with each token.

#### How it works: How token order is added

Each token receives an additional input that carries information about its position in the sequence.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> The dominant approach for preserving information about the order of tokens is to represent this to the model as an additional input associated with each token.

The position inputs may be learned during training or fixed in advance.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> These inputs are called positional encodings, and they can either be learned or fixed a priori.

In the fixed scheme described here, sine and cosine functions produce a positional encoding matrix with one row per sequence position and columns for different encoding dimensions.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> In the positional embedding matrix $\mathbf{P}$, rows correspond to positions within a sequence and columns represent different positional encoding dimensions.

The positional encoding matrix is added to the matrix containing the token inputs.

Source: `d2l-self-attention`

> The positional encoding outputs $\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{P}$ using a positional embedding matrix $\mathbf{P} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ of the same shape

<a id="softmax-ordering"></a>

### Softmax Preserves Ordering

Softmax preserves the ordering of its inputs, so the largest logit identifies the highest-probability class.

Source: `d2l-softmax-regression`

> Moreover, because the softmax operation preserves the ordering among its arguments, we do not need to compute the softmax to determine which class has been assigned the highest probability.

#### What it is: Softmax Keeps Values in the Same Order

Softmax preserves the ordering of its input values.

Source: `d2l-softmax-regression`

> the softmax operation preserves the ordering among its arguments

You can identify the class with the highest probability without calculating softmax.

Source: `d2l-softmax-regression`

> we do not need to compute the softmax to determine which class has been assigned the highest probability

#### Why it exists: Pick the most likely class with less work

Softmax keeps class scores in the same order, so the largest score still points to the most likely class.

Source: `d2l-softmax-regression`

> Note that the largest coordinate of $\mathbf{o}$ corresponds to the most likely class according to $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$.

Because that order does not change, you can identify the highest-probability class without calculating softmax.

Source: `d2l-softmax-regression`

> Moreover, because the softmax operation preserves the ordering among its arguments, we do not need to compute the softmax to determine which class has been assigned the highest probability.

#### How it works: How the Largest Score Stays on Top

Softmax preserves the order of the input scores.

Source: `d2l-softmax-regression`

> the softmax operation preserves the ordering among its arguments

As a result, the largest input score points to the class with the highest probability.

Source: `d2l-softmax-regression`

> Note that the largest coordinate of $\mathbf{o}$ corresponds to the most likely class according to $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$.

You can therefore find the highest-probability class without calculating softmax.

Source: `d2l-softmax-regression`

> we do not need to compute the softmax to determine which class has been assigned the highest probability.

<a id="vector-norm"></a>

### Vector Norm

A vector norm expresses the magnitude or size of a vector.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> Informally, the norm of a vector tells us how big it is.

#### What it is: How Big a Vector Is

A vector norm tells us how big a vector is.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> Informally, the norm of a vector tells us how big it is.

This size refers to the magnitude of the vector’s entries, not how many entries it has.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> Here, we are employing a notion of size that concerns the magnitude of a vector's components (not its dimensionality).

The ℓ2 norm is the square root of the sum of the squares of a vector’s entries.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> The Euclidean norm that we all learned in elementary school geometry when calculating the hypotenuse of a right triangle is the square root of the sum of squares of a vector's elements.

The ℓ1 norm adds the absolute values of a vector’s entries.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> By definition, the $\ell_1$ norm sums the absolute values of a vector's elements:

#### Why it exists: Why We Need One Number for Size and Distance

A vector norm gives a way to capture how large a vector or matrix is.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> Norms capture various notions of the magnitude of a vector (or matrix)

Different norms are useful because there is more than one meaningful way to measure size.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> Many functions are valid norms and different norms encode different notions of size.

Norms can measure how far apart two vectors are by measuring the size of their difference.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> are commonly applied to the difference of two vectors to measure their distance apart.

This matters in deep learning because distances used as learning goals are often expressed as norms.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> These distances, which constitute the objectives of deep learning algorithms, are often expressed as norms.

#### How it works: How a Vector’s Size Is Calculated

For the ℓ₂ norm, square each element, add the squares, and take the square root.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> The Euclidean norm that we all learned in elementary school geometry when calculating the hypotenuse of a right triangle is the square root of the sum of squares of a vector's elements.

For the ℓ₁ norm, take the absolute value of each element and add those values.

Source: `d2l-linear-algebra`

> By definition, the $\ell_1$ norm sums the absolute values of a vector's elements:
